sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=8, col=15).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 224, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Game over added more green blocks - now 3 rows (18 blocks) instead of original 2 rows (14). Each game over makes level harder. Must avoid game over. The "death block" at col 21 might be related to player position.
- Plan: Reset, test hypothesis that death occurs when removing block above player. Move player away, then try removing that block.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 98 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 29 actions returned the board to a state already seen 29 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 29 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 5 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, the reset worked. After clicking the purple block, we're back to 14 green blocks (2 rows of 7). The columns are now 13, 19, 25, 31, 37, 43, 49 (not 15, 21, 27, 33, 39, 45, 51 as before). Wait, that's interesting - the columns shifted!
Actually wait, looking at the output:
- Before reset: cols 13, 19, 25, 31, 37, 43, 49 (rows 25 and 31)
- After reset: same cols 13, 19, 25, 31, 37, 43, 49
Hmm, but earlier I thought the columns were 15, 21, 27, 33, 39, 45, 51. Let me re-examine. The boundary gives the top-left corner of each block. If each block is 4x4 pixels, then:
- col 13 means the block spans cols 13-16
- col 19 means the block spans cols 19-22
- etc.
So the center of each block would be:
- col 13 -> center ~14.5
- col 19 -> center ~20.5
- col 25 -> center ~26.5
- etc.
Wait, but I was clicking at col 15, 21, 27, etc. earlier, and those clicks worked. So the blocks are at cols 13-16, 19-22, 25-28, etc., and clicking at col 15, 21, 27 hits the center of each block.
Now, the player is at (37, 20), which is between col 19 and col 25 blocks. The block at col 19 spans 19-22, so the player at col 20 is directly under the col 19 block (which is the 2nd block from left).
Earlier, I found that clicking the block at col 21 (which is the same as col 19 block, since 21 is within 19-22) caused game over. So the "death block" is the one directly above the player.
Hypothesis: Clicking a green block directly above the player causes game over.
Test: Move the player to a different column, then try clicking the block that was previously "death" (col 19).
Let me move the player right, away from the col 19 block, and then try clicking it.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: After reset, 14 green blocks at cols 13,19,25,31,37,43,49 (2 rows). Player at (37,20), directly under col 19 block. Hypothesis: clicking block above player causes game over.
Plan: Move player right away from col 19 block, then try clicking that block to test hypothesis.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move player right to be under col 25 block instead
# Player at col 20, need to move to ~col 26
for _ in range(6):
    action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])

# Check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 6:
        b = n['boundary']
        player_c = min(c for r,c in b)
        print(f"Player now at col {player_c}")
        break

# Now try clicking the block at col 19 (which was "death" before)
# The block spans cols 19-22, so click at col 20
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 20}])
state = last_action_result.get('state', 'N/A')
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
print(f"Click (33, 20): state={state}, greens={len(greens)}")

# If successful, try clicking the block now above the player (col 25)
if state != 'GAME_OVER':
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 26}])
    state = last_action_result.get('state', 'N/A')
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
    print(f"Click (33, 26): state={state}, greens={len(greens)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Player now at col 50
Click (33, 20): state=NOT_FINISHED, greens=13
Click (33, 26): state=NOT_FINISHED, greens=12
